title: Endometrial Cancer
oneLiner: Malignancy of uterine lining, typically in postmenopausal women with abnormal bleeding; most common gynaecological cancer in developed nations.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**
A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman, para 2, presents to gynaecology clinic reporting “spotting” for the past 3 months — the sensation of a damp perineum but only a few drops of bright red blood when wiping. She has a BMI of 34, a history of type 2 diabetes for 8 years, and no prior hormone replacement therapy. Her pelvic exam is normal; speculum shows a small amount of blood oozing from the cervical os, no cervical lesion. A transvaginal ultrasound reveals an endometrial thickness of 12 mm (normally <5 mm in postmenopause). Endometrial biopsy returns “endometrioid adenocarcinoma, grade 1.”

**Dissect the Vignette:**
- **Postmenopausal bleeding** – Any bleeding >12 months after menopause is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise.
- **Age >60** – Peak incidence 60–70 years; risk rises with age.
- **Obesity (BMI 34)** – Adipose tissue converts androstenedione to oestrone via aromatase, driving unopposed oestrogen exposure.
- **Diabetes** – Insulin resistance is linked to altered oestrogen metabolism and higher risk.
- **Nulliparity / late menopause** – Both increase number of menstrual cycles → more oestrogen exposure time.
- **Normal speculum / bimanual exam** – Cancer is inside the uterus; no cervical lesion needed.
- **Endometrial thickness >5 mm** – In postmenopause, any thickness >4-5 mm on TVUS requires biopsy.
- **Low-grade endometrioid histology** – Most common (80%) and usually oestrogen-driven; prognosis excellent when caught early.

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**
- **Young woman with menorrhagia / intermenstrual bleeding** – Consider endometrioid cancer if she has PCOS, obesity, or Lynch syndrome (hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer).
- **Incidental finding on imaging** – “Thickened endometrium” in asymptomatic woman on CT or MRI for other reasons → still needs workup.
- **Purulent discharge (pyometra)** – If the cancer obstructs the cervix, infection may build up behind it; think of endometrial cancer when a postmenopausal woman has foul discharge with no obvious cervical lesion.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**
Endometrial cancer arises from the inner lining of the uterus (endometrium). The vast majority are adenocarcinomas, and in over 80% they are driven by unopposed oestrogen stimulation (type I). Type II (serous, clear cell) are not oestrogen driven, arise in older women, and carry a worse prognosis.

**Why this matters clinically:**
- **Frequency** – Most common gynaecologic malignancy in the UK, US, and Australia; lifetime risk ~3%.
- **Stakes** – >90% 5-year survival for stage I disease, but only ~20% with distant metastases. Early detection via bleeding → cure.
- **Pitfalls** – Attributing postmenopausal bleeding to “HRT” or “atrophic vaginitis” without scanning or biopsy is a classic med school exam mistake — and a real-life medicolegal risk.
- **Dual pathway concept** – Type I vs type II explains why some endometrial cancers are low-grade and others aggressive; influences management and prognosis.

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The Story:**
The endometrium is a dynamic tissue that thickens with oestrogen (proliferative phase) and matures/sheds under progesterone. Anything that gives prolonged, unopposed oestrogen stimulation (no progesterone to counteract it) drives endometrial hyperplasia → atypia → carcinoma.

**Mechanism to Signs:**
- **Unopposed oestrogen** → Mitogenic drive on endometrial glands → increased gland-to-stroma ratio → thickened endometrium on TVUS.
- **As hyperplasia becomes cancer**, the tissue loses normal architecture and becomes fragile and poorly vascularised → **bleeding** (the classic symptom) — even minor trauma (e.g., speculum insertion) can cause ooze.
- **Oestrogen is produced peripherally** in fat cells from androstenedione (a weak adrenal androgen). More fat → more conversion → more oestrogen. This explains **obesity as the strongest modifiable risk factor**.
- **Type II (serous, clear cell)** are not oestrogen driven. Instead they have TP53 mutations (serous) or are associated with Lynch syndrome. They are more likely to present at advanced stage, often with **thickened endometrium but also often with peritoneal spread** causing malignant ascites or omental cake.

**Connecting to Pattern Recognition:**
- Postmenopausal **bleeding** = the fragile tumour bleeds when the cervix is slightly disturbed or due to tissue necrosis.
- **Normal pelvic exam** = the tumour is inside the uterine cavity, not protruding through the cervix (that would be a cervical lesion, which is a different cancer).
- **Low-grade endometrioid type** = the tumour retains some features of normal endometrial tissue; it is still oestrogen-dependent and usually confined to the uterus.

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Category | Examples | Stem Clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Endogenous oestrogen excess** | Obesity (↑aromatase), chronic anovulation (PCOS), oestrogen-secreting tumours | “Obese woman with irregular bleeding” |
| **Exogenous oestrogen** | Unopposed oestrogen HRT (without progesterone), tamoxifen (partial agonist in endometrium) | “History of HRT without progestin” or “Tamoxifen for breast cancer” |
| **Aging / reproductive history** | Early menarche, late menopause (>55y), nulliparity | “Late menopause, never pregnant” |
| **Genetic syndromes** | Lynch syndrome (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2 mutations) | “Family history of colon cancer, endometrial cancer age <50” |
| **Metabolic** | Diabetes, hypertension (both linked to the metabolic syndrome cluster) | “Obese, hypertensive, diabetic woman” |
| **Radiation** | Prior pelvic radiotherapy (rare) | “History of pelvic radiation for cervical cancer” |

**Most common cause:** Obesity (drives >50% of endometrial cancers). The rest are mostly from unopposed oestrogen (endogenous or exogenous). Lynch syndrome accounts for maybe 2-5% but is important because of implications for other cancers.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **Gold standard** – Histological confirmation from endometrial biopsy (Pipelle or hysteroscopic directed biopsy).
- **No formal scoring system for diagnosis**; staging uses **FIGO** (surgical-pathologic staging: based on myometrial invasion, cervical involvement, adnexal spread, nodal status).

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS)** | Endometrial thickness (ET) | First-line imaging in postmenopausal bleeding. If ET ≤4-5 mm and endometrium regular → low risk (negative predictive value >99%). If ET >5 mm → must biopsy. | “Thickened endometrium” is NOT diagnostic of cancer; it is a screening trigger. The thinner the better. |
| **Endometrial biopsy (Pipelle)** | Tissue diagnosis (grade, histotype) | Confirm or exclude cancer. Can miss focal lesions (10-15% false-negative). If negative but high suspicion → hysteroscopy. | “Adenocarcinoma, endometrioid type” – confirms cancer. Grade 1-3. |
| **Hysteroscopy with D&C** | Direct visual inspection, curettage | Used if Pipelle is inadequate, inadequate sampling, or suspicion of polyp/mucosal lesion. | Gives full specimen; can rule out other pathology. |
| **MRI (pelvis)** | Myometrial invasion depth, cervical involvement, lymph nodes | To stage preoperatively (especially if high-grade or suspected deep invasion). | <50% invasion = stage IA, >50% = IB. Cervical involvement = stage II. |
| **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis** | Distant mets, thoracic nodes, liver, spleen | If advanced disease suspected (poorly differentiated, serous, high-grade) or for staging. | Nodal enlargement, lung mets, omental cake. |
| **CA-125** | Tumour marker (non-specific) | Not diagnostic; may be elevated in advanced disease or serous type; used to monitor recurrence. | >35 U/mL suggests possible extrauterine spread, but false positives common. |

**Pattern-recognition: single finding that confirms:** Histology from biopsy → **“endometrioid adenocarcinoma”** (or serous, clear cell, etc.)  
**Single finding that rules out:** Adequate TVUS showing ET ≤4 mm in a postmenopausal woman (with or without asymptomatic bleeding) – but clinical follow-up still recommended.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Recognise postmenopausal bleeding as a red flag** → arrange urgent gynaecology referral (within 2 weeks in UK via 2-week-wait rule).
- **Don’t start blind oestrogen therapy** (“just a bit of spotting”) until workup complete.
- **If heavy bleeding (rare)** – IV access, crossmatch, consider uterine balloon tamponade (Bakri balloon) for haemorrhage while awaiting definitive care.

**Ward (24-72h):**
- **Surgery is the cornerstone** – Total hysterectomy + bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (TH+BSO) ± pelvic lymphadenectomy.
    - *Why:* Removes the primary tumour; ovaries produce oestrogen that can stimulate any residual disease.
    - *Lymphadenectomy* – Controversial: routine for high-grade, deep invasion, serous; may be omitted for low-grade stage IA.
- **Laparoscopic/minimally invasive** is preferred (shorter hospital stay, less morbidity).
- **For women who are poor surgical candidates** (e.g., severe comorbidity) → primary radiotherapy (brachytherapy ± external beam) can be curative for early stage.
- **Hormonal therapy** (progestins) is reserved for:
    - Young women desiring fertility (with low-grade, stage IA, no myometrial invasion).
    - Advanced/recurrent disease that is oestrogen-receptor positive.

**Long-term:**
- **Adjuvant radiotherapy** – For high-risk features: grade 3, deep myometrial invasion, cervical involvement, serous/clear cell type.
- **Chemotherapy** – For advanced (stage III/IV) or high-risk (serous, clear cell) – **Carboplatin AUC5 + Paclitaxel 175 mg/m²** IV every 3 weeks × 6 cycles.
- **Hormone therapy for advanced** – **Megestrol acetate 160 mg PO once daily** (or medroxyprogesterone).
- **Surveillance** – Clinical exams ± CA-125 every 3-6 months for first 2 years, then yearly. Imaging only if symptoms.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- **Do NOT biopsy through a cervical stenosis** without ultrasound guidance → perforation risk.
- **Do NOT attribute bleeding to atrophic vaginitis** without scans first – it can coexist.
- **Do NOT give oestrogen-only HRT to a woman with an intact uterus** (that is the classic cause of endometrial cancer).
- **Do NOT forget Lynch syndrome testing** – all endometrial cancers should have mismatch repair testing (immunohistochemistry for MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) because 2-5% are Lynch-associated; young age (<50) is an absolute indication.
- **Failure to stage properly** – If you don’t take out the ovaries, residual oestrogen can drive recurrence in type I tumours.

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Endometrial Cancer | Atrophic Vaginitis | Cervical Cancer | Endometrial Polyp (benign) |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Typically none (bleeding only) | Sometimes dyspareunia, pruritus | Late: pelvic pain, sciatica | None |
| **Exam** | Blood from os, uterus normal size | Thinned vaginal mucosa, pale | Hard, friable cervix; contact bleeding | Normal (polyp may be seen on US) |
| **TVUS** | ET >5 mm, irregular | ET thin (<5 mm) | Normal ET; cervical mass | Focal hyperechoic lesion, regular border |
| **Labs** | CA-125 may be elevated | Normal | Persistent high-risk HPV, increased SCC (if squamous) | Normal |
| **Key diff** | Any postmenopausal bleeding + ET >5 mm → tissue diagnosis needed | Bleeding but ET thin (<5 mm) and biopsy negative; vaginal atrophy on exam | Positive HPV, cervical mass on speculum; biopsy of cervix | Polyp seen on hysteroscopy, histology benign |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**Major complication: Myometrial invasion → lymph node metastasis → distant spread**
- **Presentation:** Persistent bleeding despite treatment; later: pelvic pain, leg oedema (nodal involvement -> lymphatic obstruction), ascites (peritoneal spread), vaginal metastasis (bleeding from secondary site).
- **Key signs:** Palpable adnexal masses (ovarian involvement), nodular feel on rectal exam (uterosacral ligament), pleural effusion (lung metastasis).
- **What to do:** Stage with CT/PET, refer to gynae-oncology; consider palliative radiation or chemo.

**Complication: Progression to cervical involvement (stage II)**
- **Presentation:** Bleeding becomes heavier; cervical stenosis may cause haematometra (pain + infection).
- **Key signs:** Speculum shows tumour prolapsed through os; MRI shows cervical stromal invasion.
- **What to do:** Radical hysterectomy (Type II/III) or chemoradiation.

**Late-presentation pattern (exam favourite):**
- “A 70-year-old woman with months of postmenopausal bleeding who didn’t see a doctor. Now she has an abdominal mass, leg swelling, and weight loss.” → Advanced stage III/IV. Enlarged uterus, omental cake. Manage with debulking surgery + chemo.

**Complication: Recurrence**
- **Pattern:** Vaginal vault bleeding >6 months after treatment; new pelvic sidewall mass; lung nodules on chest X-ray.
- **What to do:** Consider second-line chemo (if platinum-sensitive) or immunotherapy (pembrolizumab if MSI-high). Palliation if widespread.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

1. **“RED FLAG”** for postmenopausal bleeding workup:
   - **R** – Referred bleeding? (No, it’s uterine)
   - **E** – Endometrial thickness on TVUS
   - **D** – Do biopsy (Pipelle)
   - **F** – Full hysterectomy if cancer
   - **L** – Look for Lynch (MMR testing)
   - **A** – Adnexal removal
   - **G** – Grade and stage

2. **OBESITY + PMB → Endometrial cancer** (mnemonic for risk: **O**estrogen, **B**leeding, **E**ndometrium, **S**urgery, **I**nsulin resistance, **T**umour, **Y**ears after menopause)

**Clinical pearls:**
- **The thinner the better**: In postmenopausal women, endometrial thickness ≤4 mm on TVUS has a negative predictive value >99% for cancer. Don’t biopsy if asymptomatic and thin.
- **Tamoxifen increases risk**: Women on tamoxifen (for breast cancer) have a 2-3x increased risk of endometrial cancer; any bleeding in these patients requires aggressive workup (transvaginal ultrasound + biopsy even if ET borderline).
- **Lynch syndrome is underdiagnosed**: All endometrial cancers should have MMR testing by IHC. If lost expression of MSH2 or MSH6 (not MLH1) → refer for genetic counselling. This affects the patient and family.
- **Low-grade endometrioid is curable**: >95% 5-year survival for stage IA. Don’t over-aggressively treat with chemo/RT.
- **Bleeding after HRT cessation**: A woman on sequential combined HRT who bleeds after stopping HRT for 6 months? She may still have a missed endometrial cancer – the HRT kept the endometrium from showing the typical pattern. Biopsy is needed.
- **Don’t skip the pelvic MRI**: In high-grade or deep invasion, it upstages disease and changes surgical approach (type of hysterectomy, need for lymphadenectomy).
- **CA-125 is not diagnostic**: It can be normal in early disease; elevated in advanced, but also in endometriosis, ovarian cysts, liver disease. Use for trend, not diagnosis.

**Common exam / ward mistakes:**
- Mistaking **postmenopausal bleeding** for “a tiny bit of spotting from HRT” → wait for it to settle → leads to delayed diagnosis.
- **Ordering a CT first** in a patient with PMB → CT can miss small endometrial lesions; always start with TVUS.
- **Biopsying the cervix** when the lesion is endometrial → missing the diagnosis.
- **Giving antibiotics for “pelvic infection”** without scanning – pyometra can be due to obstructing tumour.

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call senior (within same day):**
- Heavy vaginal bleeding with haemodynamic instability (tachycardia >100, BP <90/60, Hb drop >2 g/dL)
- Suspicion of uterine perforation during biopsy (pain, hypotension, free fluid on scan)
- Failed biopsy / unable to pass Pipelle due to cervical stenosis → needs hysteroscopy under GA
- New neurological symptoms (e.g., leg weakness, headache) – possible brain metastasis

**When to consider ICU / critical care:**
- **Sepsis from pyometra** – foul discharge, fever, raised WCC/lactate, NEWS2 ≥7 → broad-spectrum IV antibiotics + empyema drainage (dilatation + suction)
- **Massive haemorrhage** (unstable, Hb <7, on >4 units blood) – consider uterine artery embolisation, call interventional radiology +/- liaise with anaesthetics for ICU for transfusion support
- **Advanced stage with acute kidney injury** from ureteric obstruction – needs nephrostomy or stenting

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** → escalate to ward senior; **≥ 7** → consider critical care outreach
- **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** in a bleeding patient suggests hypoperfusion → need fluid resuscitation and consider transfusion
- **GCS < 13** or new focal neurology (suggests brain metastasis or hypercalcaemia of malignancy) → urgent CT head and senior review
- **Blood pressure < 90/60** refractory to fluids → consider massive transfusion protocol (if bleeding) and senior surgeon for haemostasis

**Oncology-specific escalation:**
- New onset of **haemoptysis, shortness of breath, or hoarseness** – possible pulmonary embolism or lung metastasis → CT PA and oncology review
- **New onset ascites or pleural effusion** – suspect peritoneal spread → arrange diagnostic paracentesis or thoracocentesis, start tumour lysis precautions if initiating chemo

**Remember:** Endometrial cancer is a curable cancer when caught early. The escalation is mostly about managing acute complications (bleeding, infection, obstruction) rather than the cancer itself. Your role as FY1 is to recognise the pattern, arrange the right tests, and get the senior to plan definitive treatment.

rawMarkdown:
## Endometrial Cancer

**One-liner:** Malignancy of uterine lining, typically in postmenopausal women with abnormal bleeding; most common gynaecological cancer in developed nations.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**
A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman, para 2, presents to gynaecology clinic reporting “spotting” for the past 3 months — the sensation of a damp perineum but only a few drops of bright red blood when wiping. She has a BMI of 34, a history of type 2 diabetes for 8 years, and no prior hormone replacement therapy. Her pelvic exam is normal; speculum shows a small amount of blood oozing from the cervical os, no cervical lesion. A transvaginal ultrasound reveals an endometrial thickness of 12 mm (normally <5 mm in postmenopause). Endometrial biopsy returns “endometrioid adenocarcinoma, grade 1.”

**Dissect the Vignette:**
- **Postmenopausal bleeding** – Any bleeding >12 months after menopause is endometrial cancer until proven otherwise.
- **Age >60** – Peak incidence 60–70 years; risk rises with age.
- **Obesity (BMI 34)** – Adipose tissue converts androstenedione to oestrone via aromatase, driving unopposed oestrogen exposure.
- **Diabetes** – Insulin resistance is linked to altered oestrogen metabolism and higher risk.
- **Nulliparity / late menopause** – Both increase number of menstrual cycles → more oestrogen exposure time.
- **Normal speculum / bimanual exam** – Cancer is inside the uterus; no cervical lesion needed.
- **Endometrial thickness >5 mm** – In postmenopause, any thickness >4-5 mm on TVUS requires biopsy.
- **Low-grade endometrioid histology** – Most common (80%) and usually oestrogen-driven; prognosis excellent when caught early.

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**
- **Young woman with menorrhagia / intermenstrual bleeding** – Consider endometrioid cancer if she has PCOS, obesity, or Lynch syndrome (hereditary non-polyposis colorectal cancer).
- **Incidental finding on imaging** – “Thickened endometrium” in asymptomatic woman on CT or MRI for other reasons → still needs workup.
- **Purulent discharge (pyometra)** – If the cancer obstructs the cervix, infection may build up behind it; think of endometrial cancer when a postmenopausal woman has foul discharge with no obvious cervical lesion.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**
Endometrial cancer arises from the inner lining of the uterus (endometrium). The vast majority are adenocarcinomas, and in over 80% they are driven by unopposed oestrogen stimulation (type I). Type II (serous, clear cell) are not oestrogen driven, arise in older women, and carry a worse prognosis.

**Why this matters clinically:**
- **Frequency** – Most common gynaecologic malignancy in the UK, US, and Australia; lifetime risk ~3%.
- **Stakes** – >90% 5-year survival for stage I disease, but only ~20% with distant metastases. Early detection via bleeding → cure.
- **Pitfalls** – Attributing postmenopausal bleeding to “HRT” or “atrophic vaginitis” without scanning or biopsy is a classic med school exam mistake — and a real-life medicolegal risk.
- **Dual pathway concept** – Type I vs type II explains why some endometrial cancers are low-grade and others aggressive; influences management and prognosis.

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The Story:**
The endometrium is a dynamic tissue that thickens with oestrogen (proliferative phase) and matures/sheds under progesterone. Anything that gives prolonged, unopposed oestrogen stimulation (no progesterone to counteract it) drives endometrial hyperplasia → atypia → carcinoma.

**Mechanism to Signs:**
- **Unopposed oestrogen** → Mitogenic drive on endometrial glands → increased gland-to-stroma ratio → thickened endometrium on TVUS.
- **As hyperplasia becomes cancer**, the tissue loses normal architecture and becomes fragile and poorly vascularised → **bleeding** (the classic symptom) — even minor trauma (e.g., speculum insertion) can cause ooze.
- **Oestrogen is produced peripherally** in fat cells from androstenedione (a weak adrenal androgen). More fat → more conversion → more oestrogen. This explains **obesity as the strongest modifiable risk factor**.
- **Type II (serous, clear cell)** are not oestrogen driven. Instead they have TP53 mutations (serous) or are associated with Lynch syndrome. They are more likely to present at advanced stage, often with **thickened endometrium but also often with peritoneal spread** causing malignant ascites or omental cake.

**Connecting to Pattern Recognition:**
- Postmenopausal **bleeding** = the fragile tumour bleeds when the cervix is slightly disturbed or due to tissue necrosis.
- **Normal pelvic exam** = the tumour is inside the uterine cavity, not protruding through the cervix (that would be a cervical lesion, which is a different cancer).
- **Low-grade endometrioid type** = the tumour retains some features of normal endometrial tissue; it is still oestrogen-dependent and usually confined to the uterus.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Category | Examples | Stem Clue |
|----------|----------|-----------|
| **Endogenous oestrogen excess** | Obesity (↑aromatase), chronic anovulation (PCOS), oestrogen-secreting tumours | “Obese woman with irregular bleeding” |
| **Exogenous oestrogen** | Unopposed oestrogen HRT (without progesterone), tamoxifen (partial agonist in endometrium) | “History of HRT without progestin” or “Tamoxifen for breast cancer” |
| **Aging / reproductive history** | Early menarche, late menopause (>55y), nulliparity | “Late menopause, never pregnant” |
| **Genetic syndromes** | Lynch syndrome (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2 mutations) | “Family history of colon cancer, endometrial cancer age <50” |
| **Metabolic** | Diabetes, hypertension (both linked to the metabolic syndrome cluster) | “Obese, hypertensive, diabetic woman” |
| **Radiation** | Prior pelvic radiotherapy (rare) | “History of pelvic radiation for cervical cancer” |

**Most common cause:** Obesity (drives >50% of endometrial cancers). The rest are mostly from unopposed oestrogen (endogenous or exogenous). Lynch syndrome accounts for maybe 2-5% but is important because of implications for other cancers.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria:**
- **Gold standard** – Histological confirmation from endometrial biopsy (Pipelle or hysteroscopic directed biopsy).
- **No formal scoring system for diagnosis**; staging uses **FIGO** (surgical-pathologic staging: based on myometrial invasion, cervical involvement, adnexal spread, nodal status).

| Test | What It Shows | Why You Order It | How to Interpret |
|------|---------------|-----------------|------------------|
| **Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS)** | Endometrial thickness (ET) | First-line imaging in postmenopausal bleeding. If ET ≤4-5 mm and endometrium regular → low risk (negative predictive value >99%). If ET >5 mm → must biopsy. | “Thickened endometrium” is NOT diagnostic of cancer; it is a screening trigger. The thinner the better. |
| **Endometrial biopsy (Pipelle)** | Tissue diagnosis (grade, histotype) | Confirm or exclude cancer. Can miss focal lesions (10-15% false-negative). If negative but high suspicion → hysteroscopy. | “Adenocarcinoma, endometrioid type” – confirms cancer. Grade 1-3. |
| **Hysteroscopy with D&C** | Direct visual inspection, curettage | Used if Pipelle is inadequate, inadequate sampling, or suspicion of polyp/mucosal lesion. | Gives full specimen; can rule out other pathology. |
| **MRI (pelvis)** | Myometrial invasion depth, cervical involvement, lymph nodes | To stage preoperatively (especially if high-grade or suspected deep invasion). | <50% invasion = stage IA, >50% = IB. Cervical involvement = stage II. |

| **CA-125** | Tumour marker (non-specific) | Not diagnostic; may be elevated in advanced disease or serous type; used to monitor recurrence. | >35 U/mL suggests possible extrauterine spread, but false positives common. |

**Single finding that rules out:** Adequate TVUS showing ET ≤4 mm in a postmenopausal woman (with or without asymptomatic bleeding) – but clinical follow-up still recommended.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Recognise postmenopausal bleeding as a red flag** → arrange urgent gynaecology referral (within 2 weeks in UK via 2-week-wait rule).
- **Don’t start blind oestrogen therapy** (“just a bit of spotting”) until workup complete.
- **If heavy bleeding (rare)** – IV access, crossmatch, consider uterine balloon tamponade (Bakri balloon) for haemorrhage while awaiting definitive care.

**Ward (24-72h):**
- **Surgery is the cornerstone** – Total hysterectomy + bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (TH+BSO) ± pelvic lymphadenectomy.
    - *Why:* Removes the primary tumour; ovaries produce oestrogen that can stimulate any residual disease.
    - *Lymphadenectomy* – Controversial: routine for high-grade, deep invasion, serous; may be omitted for low-grade stage IA.
- **Laparoscopic/minimally invasive** is preferred (shorter hospital stay, less morbidity).
- **For women who are poor surgical candidates** (e.g., severe comorbidity) → primary radiotherapy (brachytherapy ± external beam) can be curative for early stage.
- **Hormonal therapy** (progestins) is reserved for:
    - Young women desiring fertility (with low-grade, stage IA, no myometrial invasion).
    - Advanced/recurrent disease that is oestrogen-receptor positive.

**Long-term:**
- **Adjuvant radiotherapy** – For high-risk features: grade 3, deep myometrial invasion, cervical involvement, serous/clear cell type.
- **Chemotherapy** – For advanced (stage III/IV) or high-risk (serous, clear cell) – **Carboplatin AUC5 + Paclitaxel 175 mg/m²** IV every 3 weeks × 6 cycles.
- **Hormone therapy for advanced** – **Megestrol acetate 160 mg PO once daily** (or medroxyprogesterone).
- **Surveillance** – Clinical exams ± CA-125 every 3-6 months for first 2 years, then yearly. Imaging only if symptoms.

**Common pitfalls to avoid:**
- **Do NOT biopsy through a cervical stenosis** without ultrasound guidance → perforation risk.
- **Do NOT attribute bleeding to atrophic vaginitis** without scans first – it can coexist.
- **Do NOT give oestrogen-only HRT to a woman with an intact uterus** (that is the classic cause of endometrial cancer).
- **Do NOT forget Lynch syndrome testing** – all endometrial cancers should have mismatch repair testing (immunohistochemistry for MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) because 2-5% are Lynch-associated; young age (<50) is an absolute indication.
- **Failure to stage properly** – If you don’t take out the ovaries, residual oestrogen can drive recurrence in type I tumours.

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Endometrial Cancer | Atrophic Vaginitis | Cervical Cancer | Endometrial Polyp (benign) |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------------|----------------------------|
| **Pain** | Typically none (bleeding only) | Sometimes dyspareunia, pruritus | Late: pelvic pain, sciatica | None |
| **Exam** | Blood from os, uterus normal size | Thinned vaginal mucosa, pale | Hard, friable cervix; contact bleeding | Normal (polyp may be seen on US) |
| **TVUS** | ET >5 mm, irregular | ET thin (<5 mm) | Normal ET; cervical mass | Focal hyperechoic lesion, regular border |
| **Labs** | CA-125 may be elevated | Normal | Persistent high-risk HPV, increased SCC (if squamous) | Normal |
| **Key diff** | Any postmenopausal bleeding + ET >5 mm → tissue diagnosis needed | Bleeding but ET thin (<5 mm) and biopsy negative; vaginal atrophy on exam | Positive HPV, cervical mass on speculum; biopsy of cervix | Polyp seen on hysteroscopy, histology benign |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**Major complication: Myometrial invasion → lymph node metastasis → distant spread**
- **Presentation:** Persistent bleeding despite treatment; later: pelvic pain, leg oedema (nodal involvement -> lymphatic obstruction), ascites (peritoneal spread), vaginal metastasis (bleeding from secondary site).
- **Key signs:** Palpable adnexal masses (ovarian involvement), nodular feel on rectal exam (uterosacral ligament), pleural effusion (lung metastasis).
- **What to do:** Stage with CT/PET, refer to gynae-oncology; consider palliative radiation or chemo.

**Complication: Progression to cervical involvement (stage II)**
- **Presentation:** Bleeding becomes heavier; cervical stenosis may cause haematometra (pain + infection).
- **Key signs:** Speculum shows tumour prolapsed through os; MRI shows cervical stromal invasion.
- **What to do:** Radical hysterectomy (Type II/III) or chemoradiation.

**Late-presentation pattern (exam favourite):**
- “A 70-year-old woman with months of postmenopausal bleeding who didn’t see a doctor. Now she has an abdominal mass, leg swelling, and weight loss.” → Advanced stage III/IV. Enlarged uterus, omental cake. Manage with debulking surgery + chemo.

**Complication: Recurrence**
- **Pattern:** Vaginal vault bleeding >6 months after treatment; new pelvic sidewall mass; lung nodules on chest X-ray.
- **What to do:** Consider second-line chemo (if platinum-sensitive) or immunotherapy (pembrolizumab if MSI-high). Palliation if widespread.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

1. **“RED FLAG”** for postmenopausal bleeding workup:
   - **R** – Referred bleeding? (No, it’s uterine)
   - **E** – Endometrial thickness on TVUS
   - **D** – Do biopsy (Pipelle)
   - **F** – Full hysterectomy if cancer
   - **L** – Look for Lynch (MMR testing)
   - **A** – Adnexal removal
   - **G** – Grade and stage

2. **OBESITY + PMB → Endometrial cancer** (mnemonic for risk: **O**estrogen, **B**leeding, **E**ndometrium, **S**urgery, **I**nsulin resistance, **T**umour, **Y**ears after menopause)

**Clinical pearls:**
- **The thinner the better**: In postmenopausal women, endometrial thickness ≤4 mm on TVUS has a negative predictive value >99% for cancer. Don’t biopsy if asymptomatic and thin.
- **Tamoxifen increases risk**: Women on tamoxifen (for breast cancer) have a 2-3x increased risk of endometrial cancer; any bleeding in these patients requires aggressive workup (transvaginal ultrasound + biopsy even if ET borderline).
- **Lynch syndrome is underdiagnosed**: All endometrial cancers should have MMR testing by IHC. If lost expression of MSH2 or MSH6 (not MLH1) → refer for genetic counselling. This affects the patient and family.
- **Low-grade endometrioid is curable**: >95% 5-year survival for stage IA. Don’t over-aggressively treat with chemo/RT.
- **Bleeding after HRT cessation**: A woman on sequential combined HRT who bleeds after stopping HRT for 6 months? She may still have a missed endometrial cancer – the HRT kept the endometrium from showing the typical pattern. Biopsy is needed.
- **Don’t skip the pelvic MRI**: In high-grade or deep invasion, it upstages disease and changes surgical approach (type of hysterectomy, need for lymphadenectomy).
- **CA-125 is not diagnostic**: It can be normal in early disease; elevated in advanced, but also in endometriosis, ovarian cysts, liver disease. Use for trend, not diagnosis.

**Common exam / ward mistakes:**
- Mistaking **postmenopausal bleeding** for “a tiny bit of spotting from HRT” → wait for it to settle → leads to delayed diagnosis.
- **Ordering a CT first** in a patient with PMB → CT can miss small endometrial lesions; always start with TVUS.
- **Biopsying the cervix** when the lesion is endometrial → missing the diagnosis.
- **Giving antibiotics for “pelvic infection”** without scanning – pyometra can be due to obstructing tumour.

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call senior (within same day):**
- Heavy vaginal bleeding with haemodynamic instability (tachycardia >100, BP <90/60, Hb drop >2 g/dL)
- Suspicion of uterine perforation during biopsy (pain, hypotension, free fluid on scan)
- Failed biopsy / unable to pass Pipelle due to cervical stenosis → needs hysteroscopy under GA
- New neurological symptoms (e.g., leg weakness, headache) – possible brain metastasis

**When to consider ICU / critical care:**
- **Sepsis from pyometra** – foul discharge, fever, raised WCC/lactate, NEWS2 ≥7 → broad-spectrum IV antibiotics + empyema drainage (dilatation + suction)
- **Massive haemorrhage** (unstable, Hb <7, on >4 units blood) – consider uterine artery embolisation, call interventional radiology +/- liaise with anaesthetics for ICU for transfusion support
- **Advanced stage with acute kidney injury** from ureteric obstruction – needs nephrostomy or stenting

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2 ≥ 5** → escalate to ward senior; **≥ 7** → consider critical care outreach
- **Lactate > 2 mmol/L** in a bleeding patient suggests hypoperfusion → need fluid resuscitation and consider transfusion
- **GCS < 13** or new focal neurology (suggests brain metastasis or hypercalcaemia of malignancy) → urgent CT head and senior review
- **Blood pressure < 90/60** refractory to fluids → consider massive transfusion protocol (if bleeding) and senior surgeon for haemostasis

**Oncology-specific escalation:**
- New onset of **haemoptysis, shortness of breath, or hoarseness** – possible pulmonary embolism or lung metastasis → CT PA and oncology review
- **New onset ascites or pleural effusion** – suspect peritoneal spread → arrange diagnostic paracentesis or thoracocentesis, start tumour lysis precautions if initiating chemo

**Remember:** Endometrial cancer is a curable cancer when caught early. The escalation is mostly about managing acute complications (bleeding, infection, obstruction) rather than the cancer itself. Your role as FY1 is to recognise the pattern, arrange the right tests, and get the senior to plan definitive treatment.
